Bill Gates, Chair of the Gates Foundation, is visiting Rwanda to learn from partners working to strengthen health systems, expand economic opportunity, and advance innovation across the country. His visit comes at a pivotal moment, as African countries navigate shrinking development assistance, rapid technological change, and growing ambitions for greater economic and health sovereignty. In advance of his trip, he responded to questions from Godfrey Mutizwa, Editor-in-Chief CNBC Africa.   CNBCA: Rwanda is often cited as a success story and a country that has made strong progress by investing in people, institution-building, innovation, digital transformation. What are you hoping to see and learn in Rwanda and what lessons will be relevant for other African countries?  Bill Gates:  What stands out to me is not one particular innovation, but the way the country’s long-term investments in people, strong institutions, and delivery are translating into real progress. Since 2000, the number of Rwandan children who die before age 5 has fallen by 80 percent, and the number of women who die from complications during pregnancy and childbirth has fallen by 85 percent. Those gains came from years of investment in strengthening primary healthcare, supporting community health workers, and building institutions like the Rwanda Biomedical Center. I am looking forward to meeting government leaders, frontline health workers, innovators, researchers, and students to better understand how they’re working—individually and together—to improve lives. CNBCA: Despite constrained fiscal spaces, African governments have been striving to strengthen their health systems; should they rethink funding models for healthcare and education? Bill Gates: There’s no doubt this is a difficult moment. Development assistance is declining, and governments across the continent are facing hard decisions about how to prioritize their limited resources. But when resources are constrained, the priority should be to invest in the interventions that we know do the greatest good. Investing in primary health care means investing in a country’s greatest resource: its people. Across Africa, I’m encouraged to see governments prioritizing these investments. I am looking forward to seeing that in practice here in Rwanda, and learning more about the country’s ongoing investments in primary care, community health workers, prevention, and early treatment—some of the most cost-effective ways to save lives. We’re also seeing countries like Senegal and Zambia establish Health Transformation Units that improve coordination, reduce duplication, and direct limited resources to the highest-impact interventions.  Last year, I announced that the Gates Foundation will invest $200 billion over the next two decades, with the vast majority to benefit Africa. Ultimately, long-term progress will depend on stronger domestic institutions and leadership, which is why our role is to support the priorities African countries have already set for themselves. CNBCA: You have long suggested investment in people and innovation. Where do you see the greatest untapped opportunities for Africa’s young population over the next decade? Bill Gates:  Africa’s greatest asset is its young people. By 2040, the continent will have the world’s largest working-age population. If countries continue investing in health, education, and innovation, that demographic shift can become one of the greatest economic opportunities of this century.  What makes me optimistic is that the next generation of innovators isn’t waiting for opportunities—they are creating them. Across the continent, young entrepreneurs are building AI applications, financial technologies, health innovations, creative industries, and businesses that will compete globally. In Rwanda, institutions like the University of Global Health Equity and the Rwanda Coding Academy are developing the next generation of scientists and innovators.  Every major technological shift creates new opportunities for people who are ready to build. Africa has already shown that by leapfrogging traditional banking through mobile money. I believe AI, digital public infrastructure, and advances in health and agriculture will create the same opportunity for this generation. CNBCA: Artificial intelligence is transforming industries worldwide. How can African countries ensure they are not merely consumers of AI but active participants in shaping and benefiting from the technology? Bill Gates: AI is the biggest technological advancement of our lifetime. It has the potential to help solve some of Africa’s biggest challenges. For example, AI can support teachers with better tools to personalize learning, identify students who are falling behind, and improve foundational skills. But technology on its own isn’t the answer. AI will only make a difference if it helps people do their jobs better and improves the quality of the services they provide. Whether it’s supporting a nurse to make a better clinical decision, helping a community health worker identify a high-risk pregnancy, or giving a farmer better information, AI should strengthen human capability rather than replace it.  I’ll be writing more about AI soon. But during this trip, I’m eager to meet with health workers, farmers, students, and innovators to learn more about Rwanda’s approach to AI. The National Health Intelligence Center in Rwanda, for instance, is working to connect service data, patient records, supply chain information, insurance claims, workforce data, and civil registration so leaders can make faster and better decisions. The Gates Foundation is working to support those efforts through programs like Horizon 1000, which aims to equip 1,000 primary health clinics with AI tools by 2028.  CNBCA: Several African countries are investing in digital public infrastructure. What are the most important foundations governments need to establish to ensure these systems deliver inclusive economic growth? Bill Gates: DPI is essential for economic growth. When countries build digital payment and secure data-sharing systems that work together, it becomes easier for people to start businesses, access financial services, and receive government support. For example, Tanzania’s m-mama model uses dispatch technology to connect pregnant women and newborns to emergency transportation, including community drivers where ambulances are unavailable. So far, this has resulted in more than 150,000 emergency journeys in Tanzania, with more than 6,300 lives saved. The most important ingredients are trust and inclusion. People need confidence that their data is protected, systems are secure, and everyone will benefit. If digital systems aren’t designed to include women, small business owners, farmers, and people in rural communities, they risk widening inequality instead of reducing it. This is why the Gates Foundation supports partnerships with organizations like AfricaNenda and Equity Group. These collaborations are focused on helping countries build digital infrastructure in ways that expand financial inclusion, strengthen cross-border payments, and improve people’s lives. CNBCA: Local manufacturing is increasingly viewed as essential for economic resilience. In which sectors do you believe Africa has the greatest potential to build globally competitive industries? Bill Gates: COVID reminded the world that resilient supply chains matter just as much as scientific breakthroughs. When countries struggled to access vaccines, syringes, and other essential medical products, it became clear that relying on a handful of manufacturers thousands of miles away creates real risks.   Thankfully, today, African manufacturers are increasingly making medicines, diagnostics, and vaccines themselves. Here in Rwanda, TKMD has produced more than 22 million syringes since 2024, secured a contract with UNICEF to procure 150 million more units, and is on track to cut transport times across East, Central, and Southern Africa by up to 90 percent. In Kenya, Revital Healthcare has shown that African manufacturers can meet the quality standards of global markets, producing everything from auto-disable syringes to diagnostics. We’re seeing the same progress in essential medicines. Companies like QCIL in Uganda and Cosmos in Kenya have achieved WHO prequalification and secured Global Fund contracts to supply first-line HIV medicines. Ultimately, this is about much more than manufacturing. It’s about creating good jobs, strengthening Africa’s health security, and driving long-term economic growth. CNBCA: The Gates Foundation has historically worked closely with governments and multilateral institutions. However, the geopolitical landscape is shifting. Does the current global development architecture require a rethink? How can African leaders ensure that partnerships serve national priorities rather than external interests? Bill Gates: We have seen what is possible when countries and institutions work together. The Gates Foundation has long supported partnerships like Gavi and the Global Fund because they show what coordinated global action can achieve. Over the past 25 years, these partnerships have helped save some 90 million lives. Child deaths have been cut by more than half globally, HIV deaths have fallen dramatically, and countries across Africa have made extraordinary progress against diseases that once seemed impossible to contain.  But the world is changing. Aid budgets are under pressure, countries face higher borrowing costs, and governments are being asked to do more with less. That means partnerships have to evolve, and it is important to strengthen and support institutions that allow countries to lead their own development.  Across the continent, we’re seeing stronger public health institutions, world class researchers, innovative businesses, and regional organizations taking on a bigger role.  The most effective partnerships are those that align behind national priorities—bringing resources where they’re needed, while African governments and institutions lead the way. Choose CNBC Africaas your preferred source on Google and never miss a moment from the most trusted name in business news.

      Medical-focused artificial intelligence startup OpenEvidence has reportedly considered raising $200 million at a $20 billion valuation.

	
However, the company is unlikely to proceed, partly because the fundraise will dilute founders and shareholders, The Information reported Friday (July 17), citing a source involved in the discussions.
That source added that OpenEvidence has also held acquisition talks with a large tech company in the last few months.
The company is now generating around $300 million in annualized revenue, which implies close to $25 million in revenue each month, the source said. That’s double what OpenEvidence was bringing in roughly seven months ago, when it was in talks to raise at a $12 billion valuation, the report added.
According to the report, this growth could ease fears among investors that bigger AI labs, which are putting out competing apps, will absorb AI application startups. For example, OpenAI in April launched ChatGPT for Clinicians, which helps healthcare professionals with documentation and medical research.
OpenEvidence is running at breakeven on a cash flow basis, as it invests in training its models for duties like generating medical notes and searching information from medical journals, The Information’s source added. 

As covered here in May, OpenEvidence’s AI-powered medical search engine is used by 860,000 licensed and verified U.S. clinicians. Among the company’s most recent offerings is Voice Mode,  a hands-free feature that allows clinicians to ask questions and get spoken, evidence-based answers without touching a screen. 
“When I’m in the ED, I’m never at a workstation when I actually need an answer,” Dr. Ania Bilski, vice president of clinical AI at OpenEvidence and a practicing emergency medicine physician at University of California, San Francisco and Kaiser Permanente, told Fierce Healthcare.
Meanwhile, recent PYMNTS Intelligence research shows that healthcare firms are putting AI to work first in areas where employee strain, patient demand and operational complexity intersect. 
The research examined AI use in financial services and insurance, healthcare and medical, and media and advertising, and found that while every sector is moving toward AI, healthcare’s pathway is distinct, focused more on targeted relief than sweeping automation.
“Yet healthcare’s narrower deployment also suggests discipline,” PYMNTS wrote. “The sector is concentrating AI in areas where it can reduce friction now, then build toward broader transformation once data and system integration improve.”

Researchers from Universidad Carlos III de Madrid (UC3M) and Hospital Universitario Severo Ochoa in Leganés, part of the public health network of the Comunidad de Madrid, have developed a methodology that uses artificial intelligence (AI) to analyze brain electrical activity during sleep and facilitate the early diagnosis of Alzheimer's. The study, recently published in the scientific journal GeroScience, demonstrates that analyzing nocturnal brain waves using machine learning techniques makes it possible to non-invasively identify early neural alterations and classify patients into three distinct biological subgroups.

Alzheimer's is a progressive neurodegenerative disease whose clinical symptoms typically manifest 10 to 20 years after the onset of pathological processes in the brain. Currently available drugs are only effective if administered in the early stages of the disease.

Although plasma testing for p-tau217 is beginning to be incorporated into clinical practice in some Spanish hospitals, its availability is not yet uniform across the Sistema Nacional de Salud. In many cases, the clinical assessment of the patient requires supplementing this information with advanced techniques such as positron emission tomography (PET) or invasive procedures, such as a lumbar puncture to analyze the cerebrospinal fluid surrounding the brain and spinal cord. These tests are usually performed in relatively late stages when symptoms are already evident.

Research focused on sleep

This research focuses on sleep during a high-activity period in which the brain carries out cellular clearance of metabolic waste, including the beta-amyloid protein. There is a bidirectional relationship in this process, where Alzheimer's alters sleep architecture, and in turn, sleep disorders favor the mechanisms that accelerate the progression of the disease.

Recording electrical activity during sleep provides us with a window into the biological processes taking place, which, over the years, can lead to forgetfulness and the characteristic symptoms of Alzheimer's."

Arrate Muñoz-Barrutia, study's principal investigator, full professor in the Department of Neuroscience and Biomedical Sciences at UC3M

"With our research, we seek a tool that enables early diagnosis of Alzheimer's, one that is non-invasive, affordable, and capable of being extended to the majority of the population," explains Dr. Anna Michela Gaeta, a specialist in Pulmonology at Hospital Universitario Severo Ochoa.

Artificial Intelligence applied to sleep recording

The research team analyzed a unique database combining nocturnal electrical activity recordings—known as polysomnographies—with data on protein expression obtained from patients' cerebrospinal fluid. This database was created in previous research led by Dr. Gerard Piñol Ripoll, a specialist in Neurology at Hospital Universitario Santa María de Lérida-IRBLLeida, and Dr. Ferrán Barbé, head of the Pulmonology Service at Hospital Universitario Arnau de Vilanova de Lérida and director of the translational research group in respiratory medicine at IRBLleida. The study included 42 patients with mild to moderate Alzheimer's and 58 cognitively healthy controls. Thanks to funding from the Instituto de Salud Carlos III, the team in Lérida compiled all the data and built the repository subsequently analyzed by researchers in this new study.

Related StoriesMultimodal surgery improves outcomes for rare corpus callosum gliomaSleep apnea and erectile dysfunction share a stronger link than expectedMost hospital readmissions after heart failure are not heart-related"Signals were recorded using a series of electrodes placed on the scalp that capture the electrical activity of neurons throughout the night. We used AI to analyze this electrical activity and identify certain changes that may detect the accumulation of proteins in the brain, which will later lead to neurodegenerative diseases," explains another of the study's authors, Lorena Gallego Viñarás, from the Department of Neuroscience and Biomedical Sciences at UC3M. She further clarifies that "AI is not intended to replace any medical tests, but rather to support early diagnosis. This allows information to be analyzed in much greater detail to facilitate earlier diagnoses and, consequently, initiate treatments much sooner."

This AI-based technology has successfully differentiated healthy individuals from patients with Alzheimer's with high accuracy. Furthermore, by cross-referencing these data with key cerebrospinal fluid biomarkers—such as beta-amyloid (Aβ42), phosphorylated tau (p-tau181), total tau (t-tau), and neurofilament light chain (NfL)—the algorithm identified three distinct subgroups or subclusters of Alzheimer's patients. These profiles showed gradual differences in their biomarker levels, proving that the disease presents different biological signatures from its earliest stages.

Results with potential clinical application in the future

In the future, these tools based on nocturnal sleep studies could complement blood tests, such as those for the p-tau217 protein. This approach could provide a cost-effective screening pathway that is feasible from the patient's own home to detect preclinical Alzheimer's while simultaneously treating sleep disorders, thereby helping to slow the progression of cognitive decline.

"Another thing we have learned from this research is that the future of science in this field relies on integrating different disciplines, such as neurology, pulmonology, and engineering. This collaboration is essential because drugs are currently being developed that can only act in the early stages of the disease," concludes Dr. Anna Michela Gaeta. 
Source:Journal reference:Gaeta, A. M., et al. (2026). Quantitative sleep EEG identifies CSF core biomarker-related subgroups in Alzheimer’s disease. GeroScience. DOI: 10.1007/s11357-026-02266-z. https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11357-026-02266-z

Artificial intelligence could help close the gap between research and real-world impact, but only if used for audience-specific translation rather than summarisation. Drawing on findings from their Innovate UK-funded project on health research, Emilie Fontaine and Fay Manning argue that if researchers accept accountability for translating their own papers, it gives them more influence over how they are used. As a result, a wider range of research, not just the work that already gets attention, would reach the people who need it.

Three point three million research papers were published worldwide in 2023, that is more than six times as many as 40 years ago. While no study has found exactly how much of this mountain of work is actually applied, the evidence that exists suggests not nearly enough. The UK produces research at scale but is weak at turning it into real-world impact, a gap the government’s own DSIT AI for Science strategy sets out to close. But before research can create any form of value, it has to be shared, not just published.

We tested this gap in health research, where findings feed directly into clinical practice and patient decisions, but the same logic applies wherever research informs decisions, from climate to economic policy. We spoke to 152 researchers, policymakers, clinicians and patients through six research activities (including focus groups and surveys). Researchers themselves were particularly frustrated by their lack of influence on clinical implementation or policy. They feel the strain: 73% rated a lack of time as a significant barrier to communicating their findings, and 49% reported receiving no institutional incentive or support.

Accessible summaries, usable translations?

AI, for all the legitimate concerns about it, is already making research increasingly available through search and summarisation tools. Access is therefore no longer the main barrier. The issue is usability: what happens when audiences who are already scientifically literate, such as clinicians and policymakers, need to share the research themselves to make things happen. Take a commissioner deciding whether to fund a service, or a clinician updating local guidance. They can find the paper and read it. But what they need is something they can put in front of a board, a colleague or a patient, and the scientific manuscript is not that. The research has reached them, but it isn’t usable in the form it arrives.

Communicating research well means translating it, not just summarising it. Summarisation makes a paper shorter. Translation makes it usable for someone who needs to make a decision. In our survey, 84% of researchers agreed that a translation needs to capture not just who it is for, but what it is meant to do. Even patients who generally want less complexity than a full paper offers, need the right context to ask the right questions about their diagnosis or treatment. Summarisation and translation are not the same task, and most AI tools are only built for the first.

Translation means finding the correct framing and words for a specific audience: which limitations matter, what the implications are, the questions of cost or access for a particular field. That work draws on interdisciplinary experience, judgment and interpretation. And interpretation needs someone willing to stand behind it.

The need for “human in the loop” accountability

When AI introduces errors, clinicians and other experts risk becoming “liability sinks”: blamed for mistakes that originate in the tools they were relying on. The same risk runs through policy. Research already shapes decisions in ways that are often invisible: a citation in a guideline or briefing rarely shows how, or whether, the underlying finding actually shaped the decision. If AI now sits between research and policy, translating one into the other, the danger is that decisions rest on outputs no one has verified.One response is that AI technology that helps share research needs a human in the loop, a term from aviation and defence, where the cost of a mistake is high. But a human in the system guarantees nothing. The 1980s Therac-25 radiation machine, used to treat cancer patients, had an operator confirming every command, and still delivered fatal overdoses, because they could not see the software fault they were signing off. More recently, the government’s own AI 2030 scenario planning sketches a plausible future in which humans nominally retain sign-off but, in practice, rubber-stamp recommendations they cannot fully understand. Verification that no one can meaningfully perform is not verification.

Which is why the right person to verify that a research translation still says what the study actually found is the one human who genuinely can: the researcher who produced it. Where a paper has multiple authors, that falls to the one who already answers for it, the lead or corresponding author. The accountability stays with the researcher, as it always has, but now with the tools and support to act on it. We found that 77% of the professionals who use research to make decisions named researcher verification as the single most important feature of any AI tool that communicates research.

From risk to opportunity

This is where the opportunity lies for researchers. If the author of the paper signs off, AI does not take research communications out of their hands but puts them within their grasp. In practice, that means the verification is visible on the output itself: a reader can see that the author has confirmed the translation says what the study found, much as a DOI shows where a paper comes from. General AI tools will keep producing summaries, but institutions and funders can choose to treat verified translations as the trusted currency, and unverified ones as drafts.

For a time-poor researcher this is an accelerator for dissemination, one that can still draw on support from research-adjacent staff. A translation that can be found and trusted puts the work in front of people who would not have opened a scientific paper, and some of them are future collaborators, users or funders. Findings no longer have to wait for a press office or a communications budget to be shared: the AI tool generates the translation, the researcher verifies it, and the decision-maker receives something they can rely on.

And that matters most for those the current system overlooks: the early-career and the lesser-known researchers whose work is rarely shared beyond their circle of peers. Verification gives their work a way to be trusted on its own terms, whatever their profile or seniority.

The researchers we spoke to want to say more about their work but rarely get the chance. Being the visible author of your own research, in a form people can act on, should not be a privilege. It is something researchers have earned.

AI research tools should be designed so the author verifies the output, and institutions and funders should treat that verification as part of dissemination, not an afterthought. AI can give research a louder voice. The accountability it demands is also the opportunity it offers, but only if the researcher signs off on it.

📑This post draws on the report, Can AI help health research reach the people who need it? Findings from a discovery study. Peelback, in partnership with the University of Exeter, funded by Innovate UK.

Getting the healthcare you need shouldn’t feel like a battle. Yet for many women, that’s exactly what it is. Women’s health has been historically underfunded and under-researched, leading to minimized symptoms, misunderstood concerns, and misdiagnoses that can take years to correct. Now, AI is starting to change that reality. New technology is helping doctors detect disease earlier and tailor treatments to individuals.As AI reshapes the future of patient care, experts say one thing is critical: ensuring the technology is used ethically and with human oversight.Keeping AI human-centric “Responsible AI means building, using and governing AI systems in ways that are ethical, aligned with human values and deliberately designed to put people first at every stage,” explains Pam Snively, chief data and trust officer at TELUS. That means everything from how data is collected and safeguarded to how AI-assisted decisions are made and explained. Most importantly, it means listening to the people the technology is meant to serve. Given women’s historical experiences with healthcare systems, trust in AI must be earned. TELUS’ AI Report revealed that women are particularly concerned about safety and privacy when it comes to AI. More generally, 64 per cent of Canadians cite accountability for mistakes as a top issue, 60 per cent are concerned about privacy and data security risks, and 58 per cent worry about losing humanity or empathy in care delivery. The way to earn their trust? Keep humans at the centre. “AI should support, not replace, human expertise,” says Snively. “For people to trust AI, they need to know that human experts remain involved, providing critical judgement, empathy and ethical oversight.” Fixing the research gapBefore AI can help close the healthcare gap for women, it needs to overcome a fundamental challenge: the gender data gap itself. “By treating male bodies as the baseline, medical research has created a gender data gap – and, more broadly, gaps for anyone whose experiences fall outside that narrow frame,” says Snively. “Without meaningful representation of women’s experiences and data, their health needs are often inadequately understood and improperly addressed.”The problem is clear: AI is only as good as the data it’s trained on. If the data is biased, so is the AI. That's where rigorous data governance and ethics come in. As a leader in data trust, and Canada's largest healthcare technology company, TELUS is leading the ethical evolution of AI and addressing the need for quality data through stringent standards that prioritize transparency, fairness and accountability – ensuring AI projects don't perpetuate the very gaps they're meant to close.Dr. Alissia Valentinis, a family physician and national medical director at TELUS Health, sees this gap as both a challenge and an opportunity."Women face misdiagnosis and undertreatment in healthcare, rooted in their underrepresentation in clinical research," says Dr. Valentinis. "AI can help bridge these gaps by analyzing diverse datasets to uncover sex-specific disease patterns and improve early detection. But only if those data are inclusive from the start." From better data to even better careImproving research and diagnosis is only part of the equation. Even with the right information, doctors need time to review it, and the time to carefully listen to their patients, especially on topics that have historically been dismissed or misunderstood in female health. This is where AI’s operational benefits with human oversight show massive potential.Tools like OpenEvidence can help clinicians quickly search medical journals to ensure no possible diagnosis is missed, drawing on a broader evidence base to improve accuracy and confidence in care decisions especially for conditions that have been understudied in women.Making time for what mattersAccording to Dr. Valentinis, some TELUS Health clinics are using AI to free up clinicians from intense administrative burdens, such as taking notes during consultations, so they can focus more on uninterrupted connection and engagement with patients during appointments instead of their screens. Opening up clinician time to discuss more sensitive or complicated topics like menopause or sexual health – an area where over half of women report their concerns are overlooked - is critical. This shift invites women to take an active role in their own care and build strong relationships with their doctors. Women should feel comfortable to share their symptoms in detail, ask questions about the evidence behind diagnoses and seek further clarity when something doesn't feel right. This isn't just about efficiency, it’s creating the conditions for more open, trusted communication and delivery of compassionate care. When doctors have less administrative tasks to complete, they can focus fully on the patient with empathy and build the trust that’s essential for addressing women’s health concerns. “AI can enhance clinical judgment, but it should never replace it,” says Dr. Valentinis. “The sweet spot is a partnership where AI offers insights and physicians apply expertise and ethical reasoning.”A more human future For any of this to work, people need to trust both AI and the organizations using it. “We want people in Canada to know there are organizations doing this right,” says Snively. “That’s why we created the TELUS Trust Centre, to show exactly how we’re using data responsibly, and to engage communities in conversations about what AI can and should be.”As AI continues to evolve, the opportunity is clear: progress doesn't have to come at the expense of empathy. For women, this shift could mean a future where healthcare is more personalized, preventive, and compassionate: one where technology helps them feel seen, heard and cared for. Not because AI replaced the human touch, but because it gave healthcare providers the tools and time to deliver it. Learn more about TELUS and their commitment to responsible AI.

Yvette Cooper, Secretary of State for Health and Social Care (Credit: Shutterstock.com)

       Prime minister Andy Burnham this week revealed his new Cabinet, including the appointment of Yvette Cooper as Secretary of State for Health and Social Care.
The MP for Pontefract, Castleford and Knottingley, who previously served as foreign secretary from September 2025 to July 2026 and home secretary from July 2024 to September 2025, is now the UK’s tenth health secretary since January 2018.
Figures from across the health sector have reacted to the appointment of yet another health secretary and what it may mean for technology in the NHS. Here’s what they had to say:
Layla Moran, chair, Health and Social Care Committee:
“We welcome Yvette Cooper to this incredibly important role and urge this government to prioritise adult social care. We have long argued that kicking this issue into the long grass itself has a great cost to the economy, carers and families.
“You cannot achieve reform of the NHS while sidelining social care. Patients cannot be discharged from hospital beds if the right care isn’t available, and that means treatments are postponed and longer waits in A&E.
“We stand ready to work with the government and Baroness Casey, as soon as her proposals are presented, to make reform a reality. And we look forward to discussing these issues with the new Secretary of State at an early stage.”

Hassan Chaudhury, chair, National Council, The Data Observatory CIC:
“Cooper has a real opportunity to reshape the future of health and care, but only if she focuses on five priorities.
“First, stop the endless reorganisations. Stripping organisational memory from NHS England and ICBs will be felt for years.
“Second, remember it’s the Department of Health and Social Care: both deserve equal attention.
“Third, pay people fairly. Without competitive pay across health and care, we won’t solve the workforce crisis in an increasingly competitive global market.
“Fourth, remove the barriers that make it so hard for technology partners, especially SMEs, to work with the NHS. Innovation shouldn’t be this difficult.
“Finally, end vendor lock-in. Open, interoperable data is the foundation for better care, faster innovation, greater productivity and long-term value. If we get these five things right, the NHS will be in a far stronger position to meet the challenges ahead.”
Nick Wilson, chief executive, System C:
“Following Burnham’s record of devolution in Greater Manchester, this approach should mean going further with the devolution of health and care.
“We all know that nationally driven tech programmes have always struggled to deliver which is why we believe a more local approach is most likely to deliver connected care.
“In this scenario it is crucial that local health and care systems are connected by technology and that shared data standards are in place to prevent IT systems becoming fragmented.”

Steve Wightman, managing director, Access Health and Integrated Care:
“By appointing such an experienced minister and putting social care at the centre of Yvette Cooper’s agenda, Andy Burnham is sending a positive signal about integration-focussed change.
“For it to be successful, social care can no longer be treated as separate to the NHS. Delayed discharges and patient flow are one of the clearest examples of this disconnect – costing billions a year – and sitting at the interface between the two systems.
“Yvette Cooper has the mandate to tackle these challenges. But to do so, we need to avoid unnecessary disruption elsewhere.
“The focus needs to be on building the neighbourhood health model already underway, along with scaling joined-up technology and shared data that connect these services, rather than ripping up and starting again.
“This would only delay the progress in social care and health that patients and staff need to see.”
Barry Mulholland, chief executive and founder, MBI Health:
“The new Secretary of State has an opportunity to make digital transformation synonymous with operational improvement, and one of her most valuable legacies could be ensuring operational data confidence is a national priority.
“Every investment in AI, automation and electronic patient records ultimately depends on clinicians and operational leaders having complete, trusted and usable information to make decisions every day. Without that information, even the best digital systems struggle to deliver value.
“I hope to see building operational data confidence prioritised, ensuring digital investments translate into helping staff and shorter waits for patients.”
Dr Ryan Samuels, director, Eolas Medical:
“Yvette Cooper’s appointment lands at a pivotal moment. With the NHS currently working through another major reorganisation, the priority has to be continuity and momentum, not starting from scratch.
“There’s a huge opportunity for the new secretary of state to back the proven, home-grown technology solutions already working on the ground, and to help the NHS move beyond endless pilots to scaling what demonstrably works.
“Clinicians do not need more proofs of concept; they need trusted tools in their hands today. This is particularly true when it comes to AI, something that the previous PM and government had committed to scaling across the health service.
“ICBs and other NHS organisations can then hit the ground running on the problems that matter most: safer care, faster clinical decisions and a workforce that is properly supported. The hope is that Yvette Cooper will recognise this and drive it forward.”
Dr Anas Nader, co-founder and chief executive, Patchwork Health:
“The first thing NHS staff will want to see from the new health secretary is a workforce plan that reflects the reality they’re facing on the ground and sets the NHS on a path to a more sustainable future.
“This has to include tangible commitments to improve on issues like the lack of training posts, and a concrete plan to stem the flow of talented clinicians leaving our health service.
“Whatever else the health secretary wants to achieve, lasting change will only be possible if the NHS has a workforce ready to deliver it.
“There will understandably be a lot of speculation around what the change in leadership means for the role of technology in our NHS.
“What’s important is that concerns over individual contracts or tech providers don’t hamper progress in the wider mission to digitise our health service.
“Creating an environment that makes it easier for home-grown innovators to scale their technologies and support NHS staff and patients will be crucial to building more secure, sovereign health infrastructure.”
Sarah Woolnough, chief executive, The King’s Fund:
“Unlike many of her predecessors, Yvette Cooper has been given a very clear mandate. The new prime minister, Andy Burnham, has said that his government must have the courage to fix the big things that politics has neglected, including social care.
“Given this new focus, long overdue need for action and the prime minister’s personal commitment to fix social care, social care reform should be at the top of Yvette Cooper’s in-tray.
“The new prime minister has already spoken about his desire for social care to operate on an ‘NHS principle’ and pointed to the billions, as our recent work highlights, that delayed discharges cost to the health service a year. To deliver this, the new Secretary of State will have to move at speed.
“Alongside this, there will be a host of challenges and live issues. Reviving the promised ‘health mission’, will be one of these.
“The prevention revolution has for the most part stalled since the historic Tobacco and Vapes Act but action across government to consider the implications for health in all policies would signal a step change.
“Steering through the largest NHS reorganisation in over a decade in the form of the Health Bill, alongside implementing a sweep of plans and strategies already set in motion such as the 10 year health plan, life sciences sector plan, capital plan, cancer plan and upcoming workforce plan, will all need Yvette Cooper’s immediate attention.”

[Adobe Stock]The Bay Area-based pharma company Verseon began designing small-molecule drugs on a computational platform in 2002. That is well before most of the companies like Recursion and Exscientia now called AI drug discovery firms existed, and two decades before the launch of ChatGPT. Today, Verseon’s website states that its programs feature novel candidates that cannot be found by any other current method.
Verseon’s website states: “For a range of major human diseases, we’re developing treatments that cannot be found through any other method.” The argument behind that claim starts with a counting exercise. Chemists have spent more than 150 years making drug-like compounds. For context, chloral hydrate entered clinical use in 1869, and historians of pharmacology have described it as the first synthetic drug.
The argument behind that claim starts with a counting exercise. Chemists have spent more than 150 years making drug-like compounds. For context, chloral hydrate entered clinical use in 1869, and historians of pharmacology have described it as the first synthetic drug. That order of magnitude aligns with enumerated screening collections of the mid-2010s. ZINC 15, released in 2015, held about 220 million molecules, over half of them for sale. Purchasable space has since grown far beyond that. ZINC-22, published in 2023, draws on make-on-demand libraries running into the tens of billions of compounds.
No matter how you count, “many are relabelings of almost the same compound or tiny changes around the same chemical backbone, such as replacing fluorine with chlorine,” said Adityo Prakash, Verseon’s co-founder and CEO. “If you cluster nearly identical structures into the same chemotype, the number collapses.”
Adityo Prakash
Both the catalog count and the chemotype count sit against a far larger ceiling. A 2013 estimate by Pavel Polishchuk, Timur Madzhidov and Alexandre Varnek put the number of drug-like molecules that could ever be synthesized at roughly 10^33, extrapolating from constrained molecular graph enumeration and capping drug-likeness at 36 heavy atoms. Earlier estimates ranged from 10^23 to 10^60.
“Think of the full set of possibilities as a chemical universe,” Prakash said. “What humanity has explored is not a planet. It is a few grains of sand.”
Designing beyond known chemical space
Against such a backdrop, brute force is off the table. “We need computational methods that design new things, the way CAD and CAM transformed buildings, airplanes and advanced computer chips,” Prakash said. For Verseon, design happens at the atomic level. “We start with a protein and ask, ‘Can I create a completely novel chemical structure that humanity has never made, fit it into this pocket and arrange the atoms so it binds and forms the right chemical interactions?’ That is what we do.”
Verseon’s own pipeline spans cardiometabolic disorders and cancer, with candidates aimed at stroke and heart attack prevention, diabetic vision loss, hereditary angioedema, fatty liver disease, multidrug-resistant cancers, CD73-positive cancers and metastasis. Every program lists two named candidates, giving Verseon multiple shots within the same disease area. “Our pipeline is filled with candidates that have properties current methods have struggled to produce,” Prakash said.
The search for an anticoagulant without the bleeding
On July 15, the European Patent Office granted Verseon another patent covering its Precision Oral Anticoagulant program, aimed at preventing strokes and heart attacks. The PROAC compounds are reversible covalent thrombin inhibitors designed to block clot formation while leaving thrombin’s platelet-activating role intact. Verseon’s position is that this separation is what keeps bleeding near normal.
“The pharmaceutical industry has searched since the 1950s for drugs that prevent dangerous clots without increasing bleeding risk,” Prakash said. “The current generation replaced warfarin.”
Warfarin, discovered in 1939 by biochemistry professor Karl Paul Link and his team at the University of Wisconsin, was first commercially launched as a rodenticide in 1952. Human use followed in the mid-1950s.
“Drugs such as Eliquis, Pradaxa and Xarelto still carry too much bleeding risk,” Prakash said. “We have candidates with dramatically lower bleeding, close to a normal bleeding profile.”
Patients taking an anticoagulant alongside an antiplatelet drug bleed more, which is why guidelines cap how long the pair runs together. In AFIRE (Atrial Fibrillation and Ischemic Events with Rivaroxaban), major bleeding occurred at 1.62% per patient-year on rivaroxaban alone and 2.76% with an antiplatelet added. A 2025 meta-analysis of four randomized trials put the reduction in major bleeding on anticoagulant monotherapy at 41%. Verseon believes a thrombin inhibitor that spares platelet activation could eventually be paired with an antiplatelet drug for far longer than current practice allows.
VE-1902 has entered human testing. Verseon received regulatory clearance in Australia in September 2018 for a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled Phase I study in healthy volunteers, with safety, tolerability and a composite hemostatic profile as the primary goal and pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics as secondary endpoints. Dosing began in early 2019. Verseon’s pipeline page still lists VE-1902 at Phase I.
In a 2020 paper in Thrombosis Research, VE-1902 produced antithrombotic effects with less bleeding than comparator anticoagulants in rodent thrombosis and bleeding models.
An oral approach to diabetic eye disease and other bets
Prakash also points to diabetic eye disease as an area where Verseon sees an opening. Patients with vision-threatening diabetic macular edema often receive repeated anti-VEGF injections, including the repurposed cancer drug Avastin and the ophthalmic drug Eylea.
“We have developed oral drugs aimed at the underlying fluid leakage that damages the back of the eye,” Prakash said.
Verseon’s compounds inhibit plasma kallikrein, a protein implicated in retinal vascular permeability. The company named VE-4840 its primary diabetic retinopathy candidate in August 2021, citing reduced diabetes-induced retinal vascular permeability in a rodent model along with preliminary toxicology results. Nearly five years later, the pipeline page still lists the compound as preclinical.
Verseon lists seven programs and fourteen named candidates, two per program. Four sit in cardiometabolic disease: stroke and heart attack prevention, diabetic vision loss, hereditary angioedema and fatty liver disease. Three sit in cancer: multidrug-resistant tumors, CD73-positive tumors and metastasis. “In cancer, we also have novel chemotherapy agents,” Prakash said.
Verseon on where AI actually helps, and where it does not
While Verseon is itself a pioneer in using molecular physics, AI and computer-directed synthesis for drug discovery, Prakash is critical of much of the mainstream AI field. He draws a line between prediction and creation. Protein-folding systems such as AlphaFold predict protein structures well, he said, because they interpolate within a dense experimental record. “Researchers will still want external experimental data to validate some predictions, or closely related data that makes a prediction more reliable,” Prakash said. “Protein-structure prediction was never the major bottleneck in drug discovery.” Two decades of experimental structural work, he added, forms the basis of most drug-discovery programs; protein-structure-prediction models like AlphaFold fills the remaining gaps, with predictions researchers may accept cautiously.
The harder problem is creating new chemical matter in regions with little or no relevant training data. “AI is good at interpolation and terrible at extrapolation,” he said. “An AI system by itself will not hand you something fundamentally new.”
Trained on existing compounds and asked to produce more, he argues, the models generate variations on what is already there. There is precedent in small molecules. In the early 2000s, Bextra, Celebrex and Vioxx were three selective COX-2 inhibitors aimed at the same mechanism, and celecoxib and valdecoxib share a diaryl heterocycle scaffold with a sulfonamide group, differing mainly in the central ring. Prakash, paraphrasing Verseon’s head of chemistry Kevin Short, reaches for a car analogy. Those drugs were roughly the same car with a new grille. Many AI-discovered compounds, as he recounts Short’s version of it, are the same car with a new paint job.
The ability to tweak on proven templates is not necessarily always bad, he said. “Could AI suggest that an old drug might work for another indication? Absolutely. AI is useful in those settings.”
CAS applied a version of that test in 2022, examining patent structures associated with Exscientia’s first three clinical candidates. All three specifically claimed DSP-1181 molecules shared their molecular shape with haloperidol, and 58% of the patent’s exemplified molecules used that shape. EXS21546’s disclosed molecules clustered around three similar shapes with precedents among earlier A2A antagonists. Two of the three specifically claimed DSP-0038 molecules shared shapes with approved antipsychotics, while 78% of the exemplified molecules shared those approved-drug shapes. CAS concluded that the candidates’ structural innovativeness “might not set the world on fire.” It also argued that AI-designed and medicinal-chemist-designed molecules should face the same novelty standard.
That result covered one company’s first three clinical candidates, not the field as a whole. A 2025 review of 71 published cases found relatively low novelty more often among molecules from ligand-based models: 58.1% had a maximum Tanimoto similarity above 0.4, compared with 17.9% for structure-based approaches. The authors also warned that fingerprint metrics can miss scaffold-level similarities, leaving room for a molecule to score as dissimilar while retaining a familiar structural core.
As the AI drug-discovery field begins to produce later-stage clinical evidence, Insilico Medicine has announced a Phase 3 trial of rentosertib, an oral TNIK inhibitor for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis whose target was identified using AI and whose molecular structure was generated through the company’s generative-chemistry platform. Insilico is now a public company, having had its IPO in Hong Kong Stock Exchange in December 2025. Rentosertib is an oral TNIK inhibitor for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. TNIK and earlier inhibitors were already known, and researchers had reported an antifibrotic effect from TNIK inhibition in a mouse model of liver disease. Yet rentosertib uses a different core scaffold from the two earlier TNIK inhibitors highlighted in Insilico’s published work, and the company reports a distinct binding mode.
Verseon’s approach is different from that of several other AI-based drug discovery organizations. Its platform, which the company calls Deep Quantum Modeling, starts with a protein pocket and uses molecular and quantum-physics calculations to design a new structure atom by atom; Verseon then synthesizes the proposed molecules and tests them. Only afterward does AI enter, proposing variations from the new experimental data while scientists decide which compounds to make next. “The AI learns from the new biological data and does what it is supposed to do: create variants,” Prakash said. “Scientists still have to make the proposed molecule in the lab and validate the prediction. The AI helps with the tweaking process.”

The AI Centre for Value Based Healthcare (AIC), led by King’s College London and Guy’s & St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, will partner with the University of Cambridge and other NHS Trusts, to drive innovation in healthcare AI using the University of Cambridge’s newly-launched Zenith AI Supercomputer.
Officially unveiled during a visit by James Frith MP, Minister for Science, Innovation and Technology, the Cambridge Zenith AI Supercomputer is now the UK’s largest AI-for-science platform with its launch marking a significant strengthening of national artificial intelligence capability.
Working in partnership with the University of Cambridge, Cambridge University Hospitals and other NHS trusts, the AI Centre will harness Zenith’s computing power to accelerate the development of next-generation AI healthcare tools including predictive analytics, diagnostics and personalised medicine. Cancer research, through the MOSAIC (Multimodal Oncology Sovereign AI Collaboration) programme, will also be a key focus area for the collaboration.
“This collaboration provides an important opportunity to connect the NHS to national-scale infrastructure – enabling the development of NHS cancer foundation models using data from leading NHS Trusts alongside the Zenith Supercomputer’s powerful processing capabilities.”
The programme aims to create an independent NHS UK sovereign AI asset that is adaptable to multiple clinical applications including improving cancer diagnosis, supported treatment selection, enhanced outcomes prediction and wider development of new therapeutics. Importantly, these tools will be designed for deployment within the NHS, ensuring they deliver direct benefits to patients and clinicians while ensuring secure national control over sensitive health data.
The Zenith AI Supercomputer’s launch brought together leaders from across government, academia and healthcare. For the AI Centre, the partnership reinforces a commitment to cross-sector collaboration to bridge the gap between research and frontline healthcare delivery in developing tools that improve care quality and system efficiency.
As the UK continues to invest in AI as a driver of both economic growth and public sector transformation, initiatives like the Cambridge Zenith AI Supercomputer are expected to play a central role. In this strategic environment, the AI Centre’s collaboration with Cambridge represents a shared vision: to responsibly harness AI at scale, develop sovereign capabilities within the UK and deliver tangible improvements in patient experiences and outcomes.
“This partnership is a unique opportunity to combine world-class computing infrastructure with strong NHS and academic partnerships between King’s College London and Cambridge to progress a new wave of AI-enabled healthcare innovations. I am excited to see the AI Centre harness Zenith’s power to explore how advanced AI can be embedded into healthcare systems safely and effectively, and translated into benefits for patients, clinicians and communities across the UK.”

Yvette Cooper, Secretary of State for Health and Social Care (Credit: House of Commons)

       Prime minister Andy Burnham has appointed Yvette Cooper as Secretary of State for Health and Social Care as part of his first cabinet reshuffle.
The MP for Pontefract, Castleford and Knottingley will lead Burnham’s £18 billion plan for a national care service.
Cooper previously served as foreign secretary from September 2025 to July 2026 and home secretary from July 2024 to September 2025, under Sir Keir Starmer’s leadership.
She replaces James Murray, who served as health secretary for just over two months after being appointed on 14 May 2026 following the resignation of Wes Streeting, who had “lost confidence” in then-prime minister Sir Keir Starmer.
In a post on X on 20 July, Cooper said: “It’s a real honour to be appointed health secretary by Andy Burnham – 27 years after I worked on the first NHS Cancer Plan as a junior health minister.
“I’m looking forward to working urgently to improve maternity services, to take forward plans for social care, and to once again play a part in supporting our National Health Service, Labour’s proudest achievement.
“Most of all I’m looking forward to working with the dedicated NHS staff, carers, healthcare professionals and volunteers on whom we all depend.”

It’s a real honour to be appointed Health Secretary by Andy Burnham – 27 years after I worked on the first NHS Cancer Plan as a junior health minister.
I’m looking forward to working urgently to improve maternity services, to take forward plans for social care, and to once…
— Yvette Cooper (@YvetteCooperMP) July 20, 2026

Last week, Burnham used his Labour leadership victory speech to promise that he would have the “courage to fix the big things that politics has neglected, like social care”.
Baroness Casey of Blackstock is currently leading a review of the social care system, which is said to become the backbone of Burnham’s reforms.
On the appointment of Cooper as health secretary, Sarah Woolnough, chief executive of The King’s Fund, said: “Unlike many of her predecessors, Yvette Cooper has been given a very clear mandate. 
“The new prime minister, Andy Burnham, has said that his government must have the courage to fix the big things that politics has neglected, including social care.
“Given this new focus, long overdue need for action and the prime minister’s personal commitment to fix social care, social care reform should be at the top of Yvette Cooper’s in-tray.
“The new prime minister has already spoken about his desire for social care to operate on an ‘NHS principle’ and pointed to the billions, as our recent work highlights, that delayed discharges cost to the health service a year. To deliver this, the new Secretary of State will have to move at speed.
“Alongside this, there will be a host of challenges and live issues. Reviving the promised ‘health mission’, will be one of these. The prevention revolution has for the most part stalled since the historic Tobacco and Vapes Act but action across government to consider the implications for health in all policies would signal a step change.
“Steering through the largest NHS reorganisation in over a decade in the form of the Health Bill, alongside implementing a sweep of plans and strategies already set in motion such as the 10 year health plan, life sciences sector plan, capital plan, cancer plan and upcoming workforce plan, will all need Yvette Cooper’s immediate attention.”

Anthropic is developing a new Claude product called Penlight, designed to accompany clinicians during patient visits. It records conversations and transforms them into live, searchable AI sessions. According to code and an early interface reviewed by TestingCatalog, Penlight is intended to enhance clinical interactions by providing real-time transcription and interaction capabilities.Penlight appears as a separate item under the Products section of Claude’s sidebar, alongside tools such as Claude Code, Design, and Conway. It features its own home screen and dedicated session pages, making it a standalone Claude experience rather than another mode inside the regular chat interface.The product is currently hidden behind an internal feature flag, and its early interface is unfinished. Several labels are delivered through gated localization messages that were unavailable during testing, resulting in blank buttons and badges on the home screen. The Penlight name is confirmed by the sidebar and page-heading code.Once a user begins a session, Penlight requests microphone access and starts streaming audio to a dedicated Anthropic service. The browser encodes the recording as mono Opus audio at 16 kHz, a configuration optimized for speech rather than high-fidelity playback.The service produces a live transcript that can identify different speakers. Users can show or hide the transcript while continuing to interact with Claude in a parallel chat panel, allowing them to ask questions without leaving the ongoing visit. Penlight also tracks whether a recording is active on another device and offers a way to resume interrupted live sessions.The underlying interface goes beyond basic transcription. Research results are structured around scientific articles and can include the journal, authors, publication year, DOI, and PubMed links. This suggests Penlight is intended to help users check clinical questions against medical literature while a conversation is still underway.Every session is internally categorized as a “visit.” The service model also includes a field for a generated note, although the current interface does not appear to display that note yet. Together, these details point toward an ambient clinical assistant that could eventually transcribe an appointment, answer questions using medical sources, and prepare documentation for review.The product’s home screen is built around a session list, with sections for recent activity and sessions that “need” the user’s attention. A persistent control can return the user to an active recording. This indicates Anthropic is designing Penlight as an ongoing workflow for managing multiple visits, not merely a one-off voice conversation with Claude.Penlight aligns closely with Anthropic’s broader healthcare push. In January, the company expanded Claude for Healthcare and explicitly identified ambient scribing, chart review, and clinical decision-support tools as potential applications. It also added access to sources such as PubMed through its healthcare offering. Anthropic’s healthcare materials describe Claude as infrastructure for healthcare companies building those experiences.Anthropic’s healthcare product page already demonstrates a workflow in which Claude turns a patient-visit recording into a clinical summary, assessment, and plan. Penlight appears to bring a version of that workflow directly into the main Claude application, with recording and assistance happening live rather than after an audio file is uploaded.Important questions remain. The code does not establish whether Penlight is intended for individual clinicians, healthcare organizations, or an internal research program. There is also no visible information about EHR integrations, regional availability, compliance requirements, or pricing. Anthropic could substantially change or abandon the product before release.Still, Penlight is one of the clearest signs yet that Anthropic is exploring first-party, industry-specific applications inside Claude. Instead of only supplying models to ambient-scribing companies, the company appears to be testing a clinical workflow of its own, one that listens during a visit, keeps Claude available in the room, and connects the conversation directly to medical research.

Avid Health at Home has completed the acquisition of Tech Medical Home Care Services, marking the eighth tuck-in acquisition for the technology-enabled home care platform backed by Havencrest Capital Management. The transaction expands Avid’s presence across Kentucky and Ohio as the company continues to build scale in the Midwest through strategic acquisitions. Financial terms were not disclosed.
The acquisition strengthens Avid’s position in the growing home- and community-based care market, where healthcare providers are increasingly expanding geographic reach to meet rising demand for in-home services. Tech Medical Home Care Services brings an established client base and workforce that will be integrated into Avid’s multi-state operating platform.
Dallas-based Havencrest Capital Management, which manages more than $600 million in healthcare-focused assets, said the transaction reflects its strategy of investing in scalable healthcare providers that can grow through both acquisitions and operational improvements.
“The continued growth of the Avid platform demonstrates our commitment to meet the rising demand for home care services nationwide, and we are very excited about Avid’s impressive track record in partnering with high-quality organizations that are delivering best-in-class clinical services,” said Christopher W. Kersey, M.D., M.B.A., founding managing partner of Havencrest.
Avid provides home care services for individuals of all ages who require assistance because of age, disability or chronic health conditions. The company has focused on combining geographic expansion with technology investments designed to improve caregiver coordination, streamline operations and enhance patient outcomes.
Chief Executive Officer Jen Lentz said the acquisition supports Avid’s mission of expanding access to person-centered care while maintaining a focus on helping clients remain safely in their homes.

“As we continue to expand, our focus remains on delivering person-centered care that empowers clients to live safely and independently at home,” Lentz said. “We are proud to welcome the clients and dedicated staff of Tech Med into the Avid family.”
The acquisition follows growing industry recognition for Avid’s technology platform. The company was recently featured by AlayaCare, a cloud-based home care software provider serving more than two million patients worldwide, for its use of artificial intelligence-driven workflow automation and integrated technology infrastructure to support operational growth.
As healthcare providers face labor shortages and increasing demand for home-based services, technology has become an important differentiator for operators seeking to improve scheduling, caregiver communication and administrative efficiency. Avid’s technology-first strategy is intended to help the company integrate acquisitions more efficiently while supporting continued expansion across multiple markets.
Kersey said Avid’s operational model combines disciplined execution with technology investments that position the company for sustained growth.
“Avid’s approach to technology-enabled growth represents an exciting step forward for home-based care in America,” he said. “Avid CEO Jen Lentz and CTO Rob Snyder have delivered on a model that others in the industry aspire to, and this recognition from AlayaCare reflects the intentional, disciplined work their team does every day.”
The Tech Medical acquisition continues Avid’s strategy of building a larger regional platform through targeted tuck-in acquisitions that expand service coverage while leveraging centralized technology and operational capabilities. The company plans to continue increasing access to home care services in both existing and new markets as demand for aging-in-place and community-based healthcare continues to grow across the United States.

PADUCAH — Charter Senior Living of Paducah is one of six Charter communities across the country to pilot an augmented intelligence device nicknamed "AUGi" to detect falls.The award-winning smart device was developed by Inspiren, a nurse-led company.

        
                
            
            
            
            
            
            
                        
            
            
                
                
                    
                            
                                
                                    AUGi is mounted on the wall in every bedroom at Charter Senior Living of Paducah. It uses motion detection and a camera feature to show the staff what's going on when they get an alert.
                                
                            
                        
                            
                                
                                    Drayton Charlton-Perrin
                
                                
                            
                        
                        
                    
                
            
        The device is mounted on every bedroom in the Charter complex and is equipped with a camera and motion detection sensors.When a resident falls, a ping will notify the staff so that they can access the camera feed and assess the situation. For privacy, the feed is blurred and anyone visible in the shot has stick figures placed over them."It alerts the staff when a resident who is high fall risk either gets up and is attempting to get either to the bathroom or get in and out of a chair," said Karen Ferrara, executive director of Charter Senior Living of Paducah.
                
            
            
            
            
            
            
                        
            
            
                
                
                    
                            
                                
                                    Ferrara said the facility has had many meetings and video chats with the AUGi team to prepare for the device's implementation.
                                
                            
                        
                            
                                
                                    Drayton Charlton-Perrin
                
                                
                            
                        
                        
                    
                
            
        Nurses on staff can decide what someone's fall risk is based on a variety of factors including previous health issues."Let's say I've had a stroke, getting up from the chair would automatically alert to the staff that I was getting up and moving about my room," Ferrara said.Residents who are not considered a high fall risk will be more free to move around without an alert being sent, Ferrara said.
        Instead, low fall risk individuals will set off a ping when they hit the floor, alerting the staff to let them know that someone has fallen and needs assistance."It's really dependent on each resident," Ferrara said. "Some residents just don't get around as well as they used to."Many falls also occur in the bathroom, so an AUGi is placed in the bathroom without the camera feature.
                
            
            
            
            
            
            
                        
            
            
                
                
                    
                            
                                
                                    The bathroom device does not have the camera feature — instead, it uses motion detection for falls.
                                
                            
                        
                            
                                
                                    Drayton Charlton-Perrin
                
                                
                            
                        
                        
                    
                
            
        "If a resident is in the bathroom greater than 20 minutes, it'll tell us," said Candice Long, sales and marketing director at Charter Senior Living of Paducah. "Staff can go in and make sure that they're okay, or, if they fall in the bathroom, it'll alert us that they have fallen."Long said AUGi is not mandatory for all residents and that the device is turned off for those who don't want to use it."Most everybody really enjoys it," Long said. "They like the peace of mind with it. The families, their loved ones really like it."
                
            
            
            
            
            
            
                        
            
            
                
                
                    
                            
                                
                                    Long said she expects that nearly 100% of all residents will consent to the AUGi program by the summer. Currently, only 80% are on it.
                                
                            
                        
                            
                                
                                    Drayton Charlton-Perrin
                
                                
                            
                        
                        
                    
                
            
        Long said 80% of the facility's residents have consented to the AUGi device, and she expects more to come."We'll get 100% at some point in time," Long said. "It's new and sometimes new things are scary."The consent form states that personal data gathered by AUGi is not part of the resident's legal health record.

Please note that by blocking any or all cookies you may not have access to certain features, content or personalization. For more information see our Cookie Policy.
    To enable cookies, follow the instructions for your browser below.

    Facebook App: Open links in External Browser
    There is a specific issue with the Facebook in-app browser intermittently making requests to websites without cookies that had previously been set. This appears to be a defect in the browser which should be addressed soon. The simplest approach to avoid this problem is to continue to use the Facebook app but not use the in-app browser. This can be done through the following steps:
    
        Open the settings menu by clicking the hamburger menu in the top right
        Choose “App Settings” from the menu
        Turn on the option “Links Open Externally” (This will use the device’s default browser)
    

    Enabling Cookies in Firefox
    
        Open the Firefox browser
        Click Tools > Options > Privacy > Use custom settings for history
        Check Accept cookies from sites
        Check Accept third party cookies
        Select Keep until: they expire
        Click OK
    

    Enabling Cookies in Google Chrome
    
        Open the Google Chrome browser
        Click Tools > Options > Privacy Options > Under the Hood > Content Settings
        Check Allow local data to be set
        Uncheck Block third-party cookies from being set
        Uncheck Clear cookies
        Close all
    

    Enabling Cookies in Mobile Safari (iPhone, iPad)
    
        Go to the Home screen by pressing the Home button or by unlocking your phone/iPad
        Select the Settings icon
        Select Safari from the settings menu
        Select ‘accept cookies’ from the safari menu
        Select ‘from visited’ from the accept cookies menu
        Press the home button to return the the iPhone home screen
        Select the Safari icon to return to Safari
        Before the cookie settings change will take effect, Safari must restart. To restart Safari press and hold the Home button (for around five seconds) until the iPhone/iPad display goes blank and the home screen appears
        Select the Safari icon to return to Safari

18 June 2026Ros TappendenSouth of EnglandBCP CouncilThe CQC said BCP Council had already made "meaningful improvements"A council has been told its adult social care service "requires improvement" following an inspection by the Care Quality Commission (CQC).The inspector said Bournemouth, Christchurch and Poole (BCP) Council's challenges, including maintaining a sustainable workforce and securing suitable services for people with complex needs, were impacting people's experiences of accessing care.However, it praised the authority for making "meaningful improvements to care and support", adding that it had "shown a clear commitment to improving outcomes".BCP welcomed the report and highlighted the CQC's "good" ratings for its safeguarding and leadership, adding that many improvements were already under way.Chris Badger, CQC's chief inspector of adult social care, said: "Families and carers of autistic people and people with a learning disability told us that people hadn't always been properly involved in the decision-making process."People were experiencing unacceptable waits for Care Act assessments, and although some recent progress had been made, some people with complex needs continued to face delays in accessing support."Badger said "significant progress" had been made in reducing support waiting times from months to just days.Its learning disability plan had also increased people's choice and control, improved access to community-based support, although problems with capacity still remained.A safeguarding review also led to changes including drop-in support sessions for staff and action plans for partner agencies."This resulted in more than 90% of people reporting that services in the authority helped them feel safe, which was above the England average," Badger said.BCP pointed to further improvements, including the reduction of occupational therapy waiting lists, from 633 people to 14 in one year, waiting times for unpaid carers assessments cut from months to days and joined-up support that helped people stay independent for longer.The council's director of wellbeing Laura Ambler said: "We have been honest about the challenges facing adult social care and the steps we are taking to strengthen services."Adult social care interim director Tim Branson said: "This was our first full CQC inspection since becoming a single unitary authority. "Our focus is now to build on this progress by ensuring a consistently good experience for everyone who comes to us for support."Adult social care councillor David Brown said: "Like councils across the country, we are delivering an adult social care service and making continued improvements in a very demanding financial environment – not helped by reduced government funding. "Despite this, we are clear in our commitment, we will not step back from supporting those who rely on us."More on this storyRelated Internet Links
